attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with a four-hour history of fever, severe headache, and neck stiffness. She has a history of recurrent meningitis, with three previous episodes since age 16. She was diagnosed with terminal complement deficiency (C7) two years ago. She has received meningococcal vaccination. She takes penicillin V prophylaxis but admits to poor adherence. She does not take any other regular medications.

On examination, she appears unwell. Her pulse rate is 120/min, blood pressure 95/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 39.2°C. She has neck stiffness and photophobia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following organisms is the most likely cause of her current presentation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features of meningitis in the context of known terminal complement deficiency. Terminal complement components (C5-C9) are crucial for the formation of the membrane attack complex, which is particularly important in defence against Neisseria species.

Neisseria meningitidis is the most likely causative organism in this case. Patients with terminal complement deficiency have a markedly increased susceptibility to meningococcal infections and may experience recurrent episodes despite vaccination. The presence of a petechial rash and the rapid onset of symptoms are particularly characteristic of meningococcal infection.

Mycobacterium tuberculosis can cause meningitis and typically presents with a subacute clinical course, rather than the acute presentation seen here. TB meningitis is not specifically associated with complement deficiency.

Cryptococcus neoformans typically causes meningitis in immunocompromised patients, particularly those with T-cell defects (e.g., solid organ transplant recipients, people living with HIV), rather than complement deficiency.

Listeria monocytogenes typically causes meningitis in newborns, elderly patients, pregnant women, or immunocompromised individuals. It is particularly associated with consumption of unpasteurised dairy products and soft cheeses. However, it is not specifically associated with complement deficiency.

Escherichia coli meningitis is primarily seen in neonates or in cases of neurosurgical complications. It is not typically associated with complement deficiency. It may be a cause of infection in patients with hyposplenia or asplenia.

The management of such patients includes immediate empiric antibiotics (typically third-generation cephalosporins), long-term antibiotic prophylaxis, and meningococcal vaccination, though these may not completely prevent recurrent infections in the setting of complement deficiency.
learningPoints: Complement deficiency predisposes to infection with encapsulated organisms, particularly Neisseria species.
Terminal complement deficiency (C5-C9) specifically increases risk of Neisseria meningitidis infection.
Patients with complement deficiency may experience recurrent meningococcal infections despite vaccination.
references: McCusker C, Upton J, Warrington R. Primary immunodeficiency. Allergy Asthma Clin Immunol. 2018;14(Suppl 2):61. Published 2018 Sep 12. doi:10.1186/s13223-018-0290-5

Szilágyi Á, Csuka D, Geier CB, Prohászka Z. Complement Genetics for the Practicing Allergist Immunologist: Focus on Complement Deficiencies. J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract. 2022;10(7):1703-1711. doi:10.1016/j.jaip.2022.02.036
Chinn IK, Orange JS. Immunodeficiency Disorders. Pediatr Rev. 2019;40(5):229-242. doi:10.1542/pir.2017-0308
Ram S, Lewis LA, Rice PA. Infections of people with complement deficiencies and patients who have undergone splenectomy. Clin Microbiol Rev. 2010;23(4):740-780. doi:10.1128/CMR.00048-09
